run.output:
Q6 Results:
Revenue: 1230113636.01
Execution time: 11 ms

Q6.notes: Correct result (revenue: 1230113636.01). Significantly outperforms DuckDB (19.28ms) and PostgreSQL (2372ms).

Q6.bottleneck: Already highly optimized baseline - parallel scan with zone map skipping and branch-free filtering. Main opportunities: (1) Explicit AVX-512 SIMD vs compiler auto-vectorization, (2) I/O prefetching on HDD, (3) Minor filter reordering
Q6.root_cause: Current implementation uses compiler auto-vectorization (#pragma GCC ivdep) but does not exploit AVX-512 explicitly. On HDD, sequential I/O could benefit from prefetch hints (MADV_WILLNEED). Filter ordering evaluates date→discount→quantity, but selectivity suggests quantity is most selective so reordering could provide marginal gains.

analysis.overall: Q6 iteration 0 is exceptionally strong - 11ms vs DuckDB's 19.28ms (43% faster) and PostgreSQL's 2372ms (216x faster). The implementation correctly uses parallel scanning (64 threads), zone map pruning, columnar access, and branch-free filtering. Primary optimization vectors are explicit SIMD (AVX-512 available but unused), I/O prefetching on HDD, and filter reordering based on selectivity.

performance_optimizations.technique: Explicit AVX-512 SIMD for filtering and aggregation
performance_optimizations.description: Replace the compiler auto-vectorization approach (line 142-152 with #pragma GCC ivdep) with explicit AVX-512 intrinsics. The hardware supports AVX-512 (detected in hardware info), which can process 8 doubles or 16 int32s per instruction. Current approach: branch-free scalar with compiler hints. Proposed: (1) Load 8 shipdate values with _mm512_loadu_si512, (2) Compare against min_date/max_date with _mm512_cmpge_epi32_mask and _mm512_cmplt_epi32_mask, (3) Load 8 discount values and compare with _mm512_cmpge_pd_mask/_mm512_cmple_pd_mask, (4) Load 8 quantity values and compare with _mm512_cmplt_pd_mask, (5) Combine masks with bitwise AND, (6) Use mask to selectively accumulate extendedprice * discount with _mm512_mask_mul_pd and _mm512_mask_add_pd. Process scan loop in blocks of 8/16 elements. Benchmark DuckDB likely uses explicit SIMD which contributes to its performance.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 15-30% speedup (from 11ms to ~8-9ms). AVX-512 provides 8x parallelism for doubles vs scalar execution. With 4 columns accessed and 3 predicates, explicit SIMD can fuse loads, comparisons, and masked accumulation more efficiently than compiler auto-vectorization. DuckDB's 19ms suggests they may be using AVX2 (4-wide) or have other overhead - AVX-512 should maintain our lead.

performance_optimizations.description: Current implementation uses MADV_SEQUENTIAL (line 57) which hints sequential access but doesn't prefetch aggressively. On HDD (detected as ROTA=1), disk seeks dominate latency. Enhancement: After mmap, call madvise(data, size, MADV_WILLNEED) on all 4 column files to trigger readahead before scanning starts. The kernel will prefetch pages asynchronously while zone map evaluation happens. For zone-map-pruned scans, call MADV_WILLNEED only on qualifying block ranges: for each zone in scan_ranges (line 198), call madvise(col_data + zone.start_row*sizeof(T), (zone.end_row - zone.start_row)*sizeof(T), MADV_WILLNEED) for each of the 4 columns. This overlaps I/O with computation.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 10-20% speedup on HDD (from 11ms to ~9-10ms). HDD sequential read throughput is ~100-150 MB/s. Q6 accesses 4 columns × 60M rows × ~2.4% selectivity (zone map pruning) ≈ reading ~5-6GB effectively. Prefetching eliminates page fault stalls during scan. On SSD, benefit would be smaller (<5%).

performance_optimizations.description: Current filter order (lines 145-147): shipdate → discount → quantity. Workload analysis indicates: l_quantity < 24 has 'high' selectivity (filters aggressively), while l_shipdate and l_discount have 'low' selectivity. The combined selectivity is 2.4%. Optimal ordering: most selective predicates first to minimize wasted work. Proposed: (1) quantity < 24 FIRST (high selectivity, eliminates ~50% of rows immediately), (2) discount BETWEEN 0.05 AND 0.07 SECOND (low selectivity, but narrower range than date), (3) shipdate range LAST (zone map already pre-filtered, so this is residual check). Change lines 145-147 to: bool pass_quantity = (quantity[i] < max_quantity); bool pass_discount = (discount[i] >= min_discount) & (discount[i] <= max_discount); bool pass_date = (shipdate[i] >= min_date) & (shipdate[i] < max_date). However, in branch-free code, all predicates evaluate regardless, so gains are minimal unless we restructure to use early-exit branches for very high selectivity.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 2-5% speedup (from 11ms to ~10.5ms). Branch-free evaluation doesn't short-circuit, so reordering only helps if we introduce branches for high-selectivity predicates. Modern CPUs with AVX-512 can evaluate all predicates in parallel anyway, so gain is marginal. More impactful with explicit SIMD where mask operations can be ordered.

performance_optimizations.description: Current mmap call (line 46) uses MAP_PRIVATE. Add MAP_POPULATE flag to pre-fault all pages at mmap time: mmap(nullptr, st.st_size, PROT_READ, MAP_PRIVATE | MAP_POPULATE, fd, 0). This eagerly loads data into page cache before scan starts, eliminating page faults during execution. Alternatively, use MAP_HUGETLB if huge pages are available for better TLB efficiency (60M rows × 4 columns × 8 bytes = ~2GB, exceeds TLB coverage). Note: MAP_POPULATE blocks mmap until all data is loaded, which may increase startup latency but reduces runtime jitter.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 5-10% speedup on HDD (from 11ms to ~10ms), primarily by eliminating page fault overhead during scan. On SSD or with warm cache, negligible benefit. Trade-off: slower startup (mmap blocks for ~100-200ms on HDD) but more predictable query execution. Combined with MADV_WILLNEED, provides comprehensive prefetching strategy.

summary: Q6 iteration 0 achieves 11ms execution time with correct results, outperforming DuckDB (19.28ms) by 43% and PostgreSQL (2372ms) by 216x. The baseline implementation is strong: parallel morsel-driven scan (64 threads), zone map pruning (l_shipdate), columnar access, branch-free filtering. Key optimization opportunities: (1) Explicit AVX-512 SIMD (hardware supports it, not fully exploited), (2) I/O prefetching on HDD (MADV_WILLNEED, MAP_POPULATE), (3) Filter reordering by selectivity. Expected combined improvement: 25-40% (to ~7-8ms). Already beating DuckDB demonstrates excellent query generation quality.
